title: Quantum geometry of connected state manifolds: When diabolic points act as bridges between eigenstate manifolds
authors: Jan Střeleček, Jakub Novotný, Pavel Cejnar
abstract: Parametric Hamiltonians often exhibit point-like spectral degeneracies (diabolic points, or conical intersections), which can lead to singularities in the Provost-Vallee metric of eigenstate manifolds. We regularise the metric by a coordinate transformation and develop a formalism in which diabolic points act as bridges between adjacent eigenstate manifolds, glueing them into a single connected state manifold. We characterise the topology of this structure and refine the rules for nodal lines governing the Berry phase. The connected state manifold restores the numerical stability near diabolic points, enlarges the class of geodesics allowing for new geodesic shortcuts, and provides a new mechanism for Berry phase computation, even along paths traversing diabolic points.
summary: This paper introduces a new geometric framework called the Connected State Manifold to treat energy level crossings as bridges rather than boundaries. By regularizing the metric at these points, the authors show that one can find shorter, more stable paths (geodesics) and compute Berry phases even when passing directly through degeneracies.
main_problem: The divergence of the Provost-Vallee metric and numerical instabilities at diabolic points (DPs) prevent a continuous geometric description of eigenstate manifolds.
main_result: The authors develop a 'Connected State Manifold' (CSM) formalism where DPs act as bridges to glue adjacent manifolds, regularizing the metric and enabling stable computation of geodesics and Berry phases.
method: The study uses coordinate transformations (stretched coordinates) to regularize singularities, analytic continuation across DP horizons, and second-order perturbation theory to analyze multi-level systems.
model_or_system: The framework applies to N-level parametric Hermitian Hamiltonians, specifically focusing on 3-level systems with effective PT-symmetry, such as those inspired by NV centers in diamond.
key_observables: Quantum Geometric Tensor (QGT), Provost-Vallee metric, Berry phase, and geodesics.
important_parameters: Diabolic points (DPs), spectral gap, radial and angular coordinates (r, phi), and the DP horizon.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes DPs have codimension 2, focuses on systems with effective PT-symmetry (antiunitary symmetry), and assumes no three levels cross at the same point.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows 3D spectra and metric divergence/regularization; Figure 2 illustrates coordinate transformations and path continuation; Figure 4 displays the topological genus and graph representation of CSMs; Figure 9 compares unstable single-manifold geodesics with stable CSM geodesics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of metric singularities at DPs, proposes the CSM formalism via coordinate regularization, analyzes the local geometry of multi-level systems using perturbation theory, characterizes the topology and nodal lines of the CSM, and demonstrates the stability and efficiency of geodesics within this new framework.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum control and adiabatic computing, as it provides a way to navigate through degeneracies and compute geometric phases for paths that traverse diabolic points.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustration of the bridge connecting ground and first excited state mani- folds of a spin-1 model in a magnetic field B = (Bx,0, Bz), see Sec. 3.1. (a) 3D plot of spectrum in a (Bx, Bz) parametric space. Red and blue crosses mark DPs between ground and first excited states, creating CSM from two eigenstate manifolds. The second state manifold is disconnected from it due to a finite energy gap. (b) De- terminant of the ground state metric tensor g(0) µν in Cartesian coordinates manifests divergences near DPs, with a schematic illustration of one of the energy crossings on top. Figure 5: Demonstration of nodal lines for fictitious CSM(0,2). (a) M0 in polar coor- dinates around DP0 with 4 DPs between M0 and M1(bottom halves of red crosses) and exemplary nodal lines between them (dashed, green). (b) M1 in the same polar coordinates, DPs are marked by upper halves of crosses, and possible nodal line con- tinuation from M0 is shown. In addition, DP4 between M1 and M2 is marked by a blue half cross. (c) CSM in stretched coordinates around DP0 (red, dashed) with nodal line structure corresponding to the other two panels.

Figure 6: (a) A conical structure of a 2-level system with h2 = 0 and DP at (0,0). The heatmap shows the magnitude of the unnormalised eigenvector, revealing the nodal line (|Ψ| = 0) passing through the DP. Two paths are drawn: a double-circle (cyan) and a figure-eight (purple, dashed). (b) The same scenario in stretched coordinates around the DP (red, dashed). Two gauges rotating the nodal line around the centre are shown: ϕ = π/2 (black, corresponding to panel (a)) and ϕ = π/5 (dotted line).
